1	\chapter{The sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence (shared slice root)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SheafOverEquivalence.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the single categorical construction on
6	% which two otherwise independent prover lanes converge:
7	%   (i)  the line-bundle coherence engine of
8	%        \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}, whose remaining gap is the
9	%        over<->restrict bridge \cref{lem:chart_over_iso}; and
10	%   (ii) the tensor-inverse / dual chain of
11	%        \cref{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}, whose
12	%        \(\mathtt{exists\_tensorObj\_inverse}\) and \(\mathtt{dual\_restrict\_iso}\)
13	%        reduce to the same slice-site change of base.
14	% Both lanes need to move a sheaf of modules between two equivalent sites whose
15	% structure rings GENUINELY DIFFER:
16	%   - \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), the opens of the open subspace
17	%     \(\widetilde{U}\), with ring sheaf \((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\);
18	%   - \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\), the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\), with
19	%     ring sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\).
20	% The project already owns the FIXED-value-category cousin
21	% \(\mathtt{overSliceSheafEquiv}\) (\cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}),
22	% built through \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\). That construction is
23	% INAPPLICABLE here: it transfers \(\mathtt{Sheaf}\,(\cdot)\,A\) for a FIXED target
24	% category \(A\) and cannot carry the varying base ring. The correct tool is the
25	% module-sheaf transfer along a continuous site equivalence together with an
26	% identification of the two ring sheaves, which Mathlib already provides as
27	% \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\). The two continuity
28	% legs that this construction requires are discharged for free by typeclass
29	% inference; the only genuine content is the open-immersion structure-sheaf ring
30	% morphism \(\varphi\), exactly the datum the project already writes inline in
31	% \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\).
32	
33	\section{The equivalence}
34	\label{sec:soe_equivalence}
35	
36	\begin{definition}
37	[Sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence]
38	  \label{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence}
40	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is an Archon-original assembly
41	  % of existing Mathlib primitives, NOT an external literature citation, so it
42	  % carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). The primitive instantiated is
43	  % SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence, defined at
44	  % Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean:305
45	  % (section Equivalence, lines 289--329): "equivalence of SheafOfModules
46	  % categories from a site equivalence e : C \simeq D together with a morphism of
47	  % the two structure ring sheaves and its inverse, with two coherences". The
48	  % underlying site equivalence is TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence
49	  % (Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean:41), and the continuity prerequisites are
50	  % the documented TODO at Over.lean:19--22.
51	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
52	  immersion \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing site
53	  equivalence
54	  \[
55	    e \;:=\; \mathtt{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U
56	       \;:\; \mathtt{Over}\,U \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}
57	  \]
58	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) (carrying the slice
59	  topology \((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\)) and the
60	  opens of the subspace \(\widetilde{U}\) (carrying
61	  \(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,\widetilde{U}\)) --- lifts to an
62	  \emph{equivalence of sheaf-of-modules categories}
63	  \[
64	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}\;:\;
65	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
66	        \;\;\simeq\;\;
67	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
68	  \]
69	  the modules-level lift of \(e\) that transports the structure ring sheaf. It is
70	  obtained by instantiating
71	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) at \(e\), with the
72	  open-immersion structure-sheaf ring morphism
73	  \[
74	    \varphi \;:\;
75	      X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U
76	      \;\longrightarrow\;
77	      \bigl(e.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{sheafPushforwardContinuous}\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\,
78	        \bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
79	  \]
80	  --- sectionwise at an object \(V \in \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the open-immersion
81	  comparison \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left}) \to \mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(e\,V)\),
82	  i.e.\ the section-level isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) of the structure
83	  sheaves --- together with its inverse \(\psi\) and the two coherences
84	  \(H_1, H_2\) expressing that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are mutually inverse.
85	\end{definition}
86	
87	\begin{proof}
88	  The construction is the assembly of three existing Mathlib primitives; the only
89	  step carrying mathematical content is the ring morphism \(\varphi\).
90	
91	  \emph{Continuity (no work).} The equivalence
92	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) requires both legs of the site
93	  equivalence \(e\) to be continuous:
94	  \(e.\mathtt{functor}\) and \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\). For a dense-subsite inclusion
95	  continuity is automatic --- it is a typeclass instance --- and for an
96	  equivalence the density of one leg propagates to the other. The project already
97	  records the relevant datum: \(\mathtt{overEquivInverseIsDenseSubsite}\) exhibits
98	  \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\) as a dense subsite of the slice site, from which both
99	  continuity legs follow by inference. The documented Mathlib TODO at
100	  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22) is thus already discharged for
101	  this case.
102	
103	  \emph{The ring morphism \(\varphi\) (the genuine content).} The two structure
104	  ring sheaves are different objects on different sites:
105	  \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\), and
106	  \((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\).
107	  The open immersion \(\iota\) identifies them: at an object \(V \in
108	  \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the sections \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left})\) and
109	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(\iota^{-1}(V.\mathrm{left}))\) agree through the
110	  open-immersion section isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\). This is verbatim the
111	  datum that \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\) already uses inline
112	  (its structure map is \((\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\,\cdot).\mathrm{inv}\)). Packaging
113	  this section-level isomorphism naturally in \(V\) yields \(\varphi\); its inverse
114	  is \(\psi\), and the coherences \(H_1, H_2\) record that the
115	  \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) round-trips are identities, proved by the
116	  inverse-iso laws (\(\mathtt{Sheaf.hom\_ext}\) reduces them sectionwise).
117	
118	  Feeding \(e\), \(\varphi\), \(\psi\), \(H_1\), \(H_2\) into
119	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) produces the asserted equivalence.
120	  Its underlying functor is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\), which
121	  is what the two consumer isomorphisms below unfold.
122	\end{proof}
123	
124	\section{The equivalence carries restriction and unit}
125	\label{sec:soe_consumers}
126	
127	\begin{lemma}
128	[The over-equivalence carries restriction to over]
129	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}
130	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictOverIso}
131	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
132	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Under the over-equivalence of
133	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is a canonical isomorphism
134	  \[
135	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
136	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
137	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U,
138	  \]
139	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the restriction \(M|_\iota\) of \(M\) along the
140	  open immersion to the slice object \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\).
141	\end{lemma}
142	
143	\begin{proof}
144	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
145	  The restriction \(M|_\iota\) is \emph{itself} a pushforward of sheaves of
146	  modules: by construction \(M|_\iota = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\)
147	  applied along \(\iota.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) (the open-immersion functor
148	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U} \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\)). The functor underlying
149	  the over-equivalence is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). Their
150	  composite is again a single pushforward, identified by
151	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp}\), which here is the identity
152	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{Iso.refl}\). It remains to identify the two underlying
153	  index functors \(\mathtt{Over}\,U \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\) entering the two
154	  pushforwards: both send an object \(V\) to its left component \(V.\mathrm{left}\),
155	  so they are equal, and \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatIso}\) along the \(\mathtt{eqToIso}\)
156	  of that equality transports the composite to \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\). The whole
157	  argument mirrors, step for step, the existing
158	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso}\).
159	\end{proof}
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	[The over-equivalence carries unit to unit]
163	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitOverIso}
165	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
166	  Under the over-equivalence of \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is
167	  a canonical isomorphism
168	  \[
169	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
170	      \bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
171	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
172	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
173	  \]
174	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the unit module (the structure sheaf-as-module) of
175	  the subspace site to the unit module of the slice site.
176	\end{lemma}
177	
178	\begin{proof}
179	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
180	  The functor underlying the over-equivalence is
181	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). The pushforward of a unit module
182	  along a morphism of ringed sites is the unit module of the target ring sheaf,
183	  transported along the ring identification \(\varphi\): the structure sheaf
184	  pushes forward to the structure sheaf because \(\varphi\) is precisely the
185	  open-immersion identification of the two structure ring sheaves. Concretely the
186	  pushforward-of-unit computation produces \(\mathtt{unit}\) of the codomain ring
187	  sheaf up to \(\varphi\), and \(\varphi\) being an isomorphism makes this a
188	  genuine isomorphism. This is the slice-site analogue of the project's existing
189	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnitIso}\) pattern.
190	\end{proof}
191	
192	\section{The engine corollary}
193	\label{sec:soe_chart}
194	
195	\begin{lemma}
196	[Over--restrict trivialisation bridge]
197	  \label{lem:chart_over_iso}
198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso}
199	  \uses{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
200	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) open, and suppose
201	  given a trivialisation of the restriction along the open immersion
202	  \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\),
203	  \[
204	    e \;:\; M|_\iota \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
205	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.
206	  \]
207	  Then there is a canonical isomorphism in the slice category
208	  \[
209	    \mathtt{chartOverIso}\,M\,U\,e \;:\;
210	      M.\mathtt{over}\,U \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
211	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr).
212	  \]
213	\end{lemma}
214	
215	\begin{proof}
216	  \uses{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso,
217	        def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
218	  Transport the trivialisation \(e\) across the over-equivalence and reconcile the
219	  two ends with the consumer isomorphisms. Explicitly, \(\mathtt{chartOverIso}\,M\,U\,e\)
220	  is the composite
221	  \[
222	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U
223	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ (\mathtt{restrictOverIso})^{-1}\ }
224	      \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
225	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{mapIso}\,e\ }
226	      \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{unit}\,\cdots)
227	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \mathtt{unitOverIso}\ }
228	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr).
229	  \]
230	  The first arrow is the inverse of \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}; the
231	  middle arrow is the image of \(e\) under the (functor of the) over-equivalence,
232	  which is functorial in isomorphisms hence sends the iso \(e\) to an iso; the last
233	  arrow is \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}. Each factor is an isomorphism,
234	  so the composite is.
235	
236	  This bridge is the categorical content elided in the chart-presentation sketch of
237	  \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}: the given trivialisation \(e\) lives in
238	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) (the open
239	  subspace site), whereas the finite-presentation transport
240	  \(\mathtt{Presentation.ofIsIso}\) must act in
241	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U)\) (the slice
242	  site); \(\mathtt{chartOverIso}\) crosses that gap. It is stated here in full
243	  generality in \(M\), \(U\), \(e\), so that the coherence engine of
244	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence} consumes it as a single application. The
245	  same over-equivalence root serves the dual lane: the slice transport
246	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\) underlying
247	  \(\mathtt{exists\_tensorObj\_inverse}\) and \(\mathtt{dual\_restrict\_iso}\)
248	  (\cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}) is the statement that the internal Hom commutes
249	  with this same slice-site change of base, and is wired through
250	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} once the equivalence is in place.
251	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence (shared slice root)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence}

4	
5	\section{The equivalence}
6	\label{sec:soe_equivalence}
7	
8	\begin{definition}
9	[Sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence]
10	  \label{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
11	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence}
12	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is an Archon-original assembly
13	  % of existing Mathlib primitives, NOT an external literature citation, so it
14	  % carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). The primitive instantiated is
15	  % SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence, defined at
16	  % Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean:305
17	  % (section Equivalence, lines 289--329): "equivalence of SheafOfModules
18	  % categories from a site equivalence e : C \simeq D together with a morphism of
19	  % the two structure ring sheaves and its inverse, with two coherences". The
20	  % underlying site equivalence is TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence
21	  % (Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean:41), and the continuity prerequisites are
22	  % the documented TODO at Over.lean:19--22.
23	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
24	  immersion \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing site
25	  equivalence
26	  \[
27	    e \;:=\; \mathtt{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U
28	       \;:\; \mathtt{Over}\,U \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}
29	  \]
30	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) (carrying the slice
31	  topology \((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\)) and the
32	  opens of the subspace \(\widetilde{U}\) (carrying
33	  \(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,\widetilde{U}\)) --- lifts to an
34	  \emph{equivalence of sheaf-of-modules categories}
35	  \[
36	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}\;:\;
37	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
38	        \;\;\simeq\;\;
39	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
40	  \]
41	  the modules-level lift of \(e\) that transports the structure ring sheaf. It is
42	  obtained by instantiating
43	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) at \(e\), with the
44	  open-immersion structure-sheaf ring morphism
45	  \[
46	    \varphi \;:\;
47	      X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U
48	      \;\longrightarrow\;
49	      \bigl(e.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{sheafPushforwardContinuous}\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\,
50	        \bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
51	  \]
52	  --- sectionwise at an object \(V \in \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the open-immersion
53	  comparison \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left}) \to \mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(e\,V)\),
54	  i.e.\ the section-level isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) of the structure
55	  sheaves --- together with its inverse \(\psi\) and the two coherences
56	  \(H_1, H_2\) expressing that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are mutually inverse.
57	\end{definition}
58	
59	\begin{proof}
60	  The construction is the assembly of three existing Mathlib primitives; the only
61	  step carrying mathematical content is the ring morphism \(\varphi\).
62	
63	  \emph{Continuity (no work).} The equivalence
64	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) requires both legs of the site
65	  equivalence \(e\) to be continuous:
66	  \(e.\mathtt{functor}\) and \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\). For a dense-subsite inclusion
67	  continuity is automatic --- it is a typeclass instance --- and for an
68	  equivalence the density of one leg propagates to the other. The declaration
69	  \(\mathtt{overEquivInverseIsDenseSubsite}\) exhibits \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\) as a
70	  dense subsite of the slice site, from which both continuity legs follow by
71	  inference. The Mathlib TODO at \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22)
72	  is discharged for this case.
73	
74	  \emph{The ring morphism \(\varphi\) (the genuine content).} The two structure
75	  ring sheaves are different objects on different sites:
76	  \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\), and
77	  \((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\).
78	  The open immersion \(\iota\) identifies them: at an object \(V \in
79	  \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the sections \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left})\) and
80	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(\iota^{-1}(V.\mathrm{left}))\) agree through the
81	  open-immersion section isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\). This is verbatim the
82	  datum that \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\) already uses inline
83	  (its structure map is \((\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\,\cdot).\mathrm{inv}\)). Packaging
84	  this section-level isomorphism naturally in \(V\) yields \(\varphi\); its inverse
85	  is \(\psi\), and the coherences \(H_1, H_2\) record that the
86	  \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) round-trips are identities, proved by the
87	  inverse-iso laws (\(\mathtt{Sheaf.hom\_ext}\) reduces them sectionwise).
88	
89	  Feeding \(e\), \(\varphi\), \(\psi\), \(H_1\), \(H_2\) into
90	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) produces the asserted equivalence.
91	  Its underlying functor is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\), which
92	  is what the two consumer isomorphisms below unfold.
93	\end{proof}

95	\section{The equivalence carries restriction and unit}
96	\label{sec:soe_consumers}
97	
98	\begin{lemma}
99	[The over-equivalence carries restriction to over]
100	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}
101	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictOverIso}
102	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
103	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Under the over-equivalence of
104	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is a canonical isomorphism
105	  \[
106	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
107	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
108	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U,
109	  \]
110	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the restriction \(M|_\iota\) of \(M\) along the
111	  open immersion to the slice object \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\).
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
116	  The restriction \(M|_\iota\) is \emph{itself} a pushforward of sheaves of
117	  modules: by construction \(M|_\iota = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\)
118	  applied along \(\iota.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) (the open-immersion functor
119	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U} \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\)). The functor underlying
120	  the over-equivalence is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). Their
121	  composite is again a single pushforward, identified by
122	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp}\), which here is the identity
123	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{Iso.refl}\). It remains to identify the two underlying
124	  index functors \(\mathtt{Over}\,U \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\) entering the two
125	  pushforwards: both send an object \(V\) to its left component \(V.\mathrm{left}\),
126	  so they are equal, and \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatIso}\) along the \(\mathtt{eqToIso}\)
127	  of that equality transports the composite to \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\). The whole
128	  argument mirrors, step for step, the existing
129	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso}\).
130	\end{proof}

132	\begin{lemma}
133	[The over-equivalence carries unit to unit]
134	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
135	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitOverIso}
136	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
137	  Under the over-equivalence of \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is
138	  a canonical isomorphism
139	  \[
140	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
141	      \bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
142	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
143	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
144	  \]

146	  the subspace site to the unit module of the slice site.
147	\end{lemma}

149	\begin{proof}
150	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
151	  The functor underlying the over-equivalence is
152	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). The pushforward of a unit module
153	  along a morphism of ringed sites is the unit module of the target ring sheaf,
154	  transported along the ring identification \(\varphi\): the structure sheaf
155	  pushes forward to the structure sheaf because \(\varphi\) is precisely the
156	  open-immersion identification of the two structure ring sheaves. Concretely the
157	  pushforward-of-unit computation produces \(\mathtt{unit}\) of the codomain ring
158	  sheaf up to \(\varphi\), and \(\varphi\) being an isomorphism makes this a
159	  genuine isomorphism. This is the slice-site analogue of
160	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnitIso}\).
